accept: MCP act_create called with literal '<id>' in title/description/accept persists the literal characters, not '<id>'.
Test asserts byte-identical roundtrip for at minimum '<', '>', '&' in title, description, and accept.
Affects act_update similarly if it shows the same pattern; either fix at MCP boundary or document the affected fields.
payload.description:
Discovered 2026-05-15 while filing act-ce77 via MCP act_create.

REPRO
mcp__act__act_create with title="x <id>" stores title="x <id>" — angle brackets were HTML-entity-encoded somewhere between the MCP request and the on-disk op. The description and accept fields show the same encoding. The CLI `act create` invoked directly with the same characters stores them verbatim, so the encoding lives in the MCP server's request decoder.

IMPACT
Cosmetic for human readers, but the FTS5 index will now tokenize '<' and '>' instead of '<' and '>', breaking search recall for any agent who types the literal characters. This is a silent agent-ergonomics regression.

DISCOVERED
While filing act-ce77 (the --blocks symmetric flag bug) via mcp__act__act_create. act_update did not exhibit the same encoding when I called it via the CLI; the encoding is specific to act_create's MCP path. Did not test other MCP tools.

LIKELY LOCATION
internal/mcp/* — likely an unintended html.EscapeString or similar on the request payload before forwarding to cli.RunCreate. Whatever it is, it's encoding the input rather than the output (output encoding would be fine; input encoding corrupts the stored bytes).
payload.title: MCP act_create double-encodes HTML entities in title/description/accept strings
